title: Detachment in Action
description: Mindfulness: paying attention, on purpose, in the present moment, nonjudgmentally.

blocks.content: Night before a race. Eyes open at 11:30 PM, alarm set for 4:45. Your brain running loops: 'What if I bonk?' 'I should have done more long runs.' 'Everyone in my wave is faster.' Same loop. Same thoughts. Heart rate elevated. Jaw clenched. You KNOW none of this is helping. You know worrying is destroying your sleep. But you can't stop. Thoughts don't have an off switch.
Module 2 gave you the framework — the chessboard metaphor, you are not your thoughts. This lesson is about the practice of detachment. Not the theory. The practice.
By the end of this lesson, you will have practiced the 3-Minute Breathing Space and understand the five facets of mindfulness.

blocks.question: Retrieval from Lesson 3.4: Match the self-talk type to its best use case. Instructional self-talk is most effective for:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Instructional cues direct technique. Motivational phrases regulate endurance and arousal. Match the type to the task.

options.text: Fine motor skills and technical execution

blocks.content: Mindfulness: paying attention, on purpose, in the present moment, nonjudgmentally.

cards.back: Noticing internal and external experiences — thoughts, sensations, sounds — without getting swept into them.
Labeling what you observe in neutral language. 'That's anxiety.' 'That's a cramp starting.' 'That's the chimp.'
Being fully present in what you're doing rather than running on autopilot. Deliberately engaged, not drifting.
Experiencing thoughts and feelings without evaluating them as good or bad. A thought appears — you notice it without labeling it.
Letting thoughts and feelings come and go without needing to respond. The thought knocks on the door. You don't answer.

items.title: Kabat-Zinn's Framework (1990)
items.content: Full Catastrophe Living demonstrated that mindfulness practice reduced chronic pain, anxiety, and stress — not by eliminating symptoms, but by changing the practitioner's relationship to them. The pain didn't go away. The suffering did. The Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (Baer et al., 2006) validated five independently trainable dimensions.

items.title: Five Facets — Each Independently Trainable
items.content: You might be naturally good at observing but terrible at non-judging. Great at acting with awareness but poor at non-reacting. The framework lets you identify weak facets and train them specifically. Mindfulness is not a single thing — it's a constellation of skills.

items.title: MSPE — Performance Maintained at Follow-Up
items.content: De Petrillo et al. (2009) tested Mindful Sport Performance Enhancement with endurance runners. Significant improvements in mindfulness, trait awareness, and sport-related worry. Effects held at follow-up — a durable skill acquisition, not a temporary boost. Swann et al. (2012) found present-moment focus was the most consistent precursor to flow states. Mindfulness is flow training.

items.label: Awareness (1 minute)
items.detail: Stop. Close your eyes. Ask: what's here right now? Notice thoughts without judging them. 'There's worry.' 'There's doubt.' Notice your body — where are you tense? Notice your emotions — anxious? Restless? Flat? Don't fix anything. Just observe.

items.label: Gathering (1 minute)
items.detail: Narrow focus to one thing: your breathing. Air in. Air out. When your mind wanders — notice, bring it back. No frustration. 'Wandered. Back.' That IS the rep. The wandering is the exercise. Bringing it back is the training.

items.label: Expansion (1 minute)
items.detail: Widen the lens. Keep breath in the background. Feel your whole body, hear the sounds, feel the temperature. If pre-race, expand into one word for how you want to show up: Calm? Strong? Patient? Present? Hold that word alongside the breath.

blocks.content: Your commitment: I will practice the 3-Minute Breathing Space daily for one week starting today.
Practice daily. Then deploy before your next race. Three minutes. You always have three minutes.
Module 3 complete. You can see before doing. Rate yourself again 1-10: mental imagery and self-talk skill. Compare to your Module 3 start rating.
Module 4 is next: The Zone and The Red Line — what flow actually is, when to deploy clutch instead, and what to do when the pilot light goes out.
